params.text:
Personal data and special category data

Personal data is any information that identifies a living person — a name, address, date of birth, care ID or even a photo. If someone can be identified from it, it counts.

Some information is more sensitive and needs extra protection. This is called special category data. It includes:

  • Health, physical or mental (this covers almost everything in a care record)
  • Race or ethnic origin
  • Religious or philosophical beliefs
  • Sexual orientation and sex life
  • Genetic and biometric data

Because care records are full of health data, most of the information you handle is special category data and must be treated with particular care.

Lawful basis for using information

You can't use personal data just because it would be helpful — you need a valid lawful basis. UK GDPR sets out six, such as consent, a legal obligation, protecting someone's vital interests, or performing a public task.

For special category data like health information, you need both a lawful basis and an extra condition. In care, this is usually the condition for the provision of health or social care, backed by law — not simply the person's consent. Your organisation identifies the correct basis; your job is to use information only within it.

params.question: A care record lists a service user's diabetes, medication and mental health history. How should this information be classified under UK GDPR?

answers.text: Special category data, because it is health information and needs extra protection

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Health data is special category data, so it requires both a lawful basis and an additional condition to process.

answers.text: Ordinary personal data, no different from a name and address

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Not quite. Health details are more sensitive than basic contact information and carry stronger protections.

answers.text: Public information, because it is needed to deliver care

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Being needed for care does not make health data public — it remains confidential and sensitive.

answers.text: Not personal data at all, as long as the surname is hidden

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. If the person can still be identified from the record, it is personal — and health details make it special category data.
